data.title: National Vision Holdings: I Don't See Market-Beating Returns Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in December 2023, I decided to dig into National Vision Holdings (EYE), a rather interesting company that, at the time, had been experiencing some pain because of the termination of its partnership with Walmart (WMT). At that time, the company was generating significant revenue from that arrangement. But because of how that relationship was structured, the overall impact on the bottom line was relatively minimal, or I guess I should say outsized in a way that did not severely affect the company. Despite this issue that the company experienced, I viewed the picture in a bullish light. Management had a plan for future growth, and shares were trading on the cheap. At the end of the day, I had no choice but to rate the company a "Buy."

The trajectory since then has been quite positive. Even though we have seen some volatility, revenue, profits, and cash flows have all generally trended higher. The number of locations that the business has in operation increased, and future growth looks promising. The stock price has reflected this, with shares rising 48.9%. However, it is worth noting that this still puts the company slightly behind the S&P 500, which has generated a return for investors of 51.1% over that same period. This is the kind of performance relative to the market that is more in line with a "Hold" rating. And even though the company has done well, shares are more expensive now than they were back then from a valuation standpoint. In light of this, I do believe that downgrading the stock to a "Hold" is the appropriate course of action.

I see limited upside

Whenever I go a long time without analyzing a company, I'd like to take a step back and look at its overall business model. This helps to re-familiarize myself with the enterprise. Operationally speaking, National Vision Holdings is an interesting business. The company operates as one of the largest optical retailers in the United States. They do this through a few different brands. They own two of these, the first being America’s Best Contacts and Eyeglasses, and the second being Eyeglass World. However, the company also has what it calls host brands. These are brands that it essentially manages. They include Vista Optical locations that are in certain Fred Meyer stores, as well as Vista Optical locations that are situated on military bases.

Management has long promoted the company as a key player in the value segment. This is for the budget-constrained consumer, which should actually make it quite appealing in the current economic climate when consumer confidence is near all-time lows. For instance, at its America’s Best locations, it offers up two pairs of eyeglasses, as well as an eye exam, all for $89.95. Two pairs of eyeglasses can be purchased for $99 at its Eyeglass World locations. To be honest with you, these numbers are shockingly low. The place that I go for glasses costs me between $200 and $400, depending on where I get them. So this does mean real value for shareholders relative to what other options are available.

Conceptually, this is a large market opportunity for the company. According to management, the optical retail market in the United States was worth about $70 billion as of 2024. However, it is a space that's continuing to grow. By 2030, they estimate that it will be worth $85 billion or more. And currently, National Vision Holdings operates as the third-largest player out there. However, that still means that it has a market share of only about 2.6%, which means that this is a highly fragmented industry that offers the opportunity for significant growth by acquisition or by muscling out smaller competitors that are unable to compete with low costs. And that's where this business truly shines.

Long term, the company sees multiple growth vectors that it can tackle. This includes underdeveloped customers, which would be those that fall under managed care, those that are outside prescriptions, and the like. It includes the underdeveloped products category, such as frame premiumization, anti-reflective offerings, transitions, progressive lenses, and the like. It includes improving the customer and patient experience through the use of data-driven segmentation, the integration of e-commerce and customer relationship management activities, and more. And it also involves new store growth that targets new markets and that invests in new formats.

Management is confident that it can continue to play in the value category while also pushing for higher prices. For instance, it believes that, through product innovation and price increases, it can raise product-only revenue by between 1.5 and 3 times as time goes on. That is significant in and of itself. But of course, there is a fine line to walk here, since consumers are budget constrained and price sensitive to things like this. We already see, for instance, that while its share of lower-income customers is comparable with the share of those who are low income in the United States, it does cater to a larger share of middle-income individuals. I actually do find this to be rather interesting considering that the value play should apply more to those who are lower income. But that suggests to me that the ability to hike prices, unless doing so purposely targets the higher-income individuals that are underrepresented in the company's revenue stream, could be limited.

Over the last few years now, management has done a fine job of capitalizing on the opportunities that exist. This has shown up in the form of revenue. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue expanded from $1.64 billion to $1.82 billion. This growth has come about pretty much entirely by its America’s Best brand, with revenue shooting up from $1.37 billion to $1.56 billion. This can be chalked up to an increase in the number of locations from 905 to 1,036.

By comparison, other areas for the company have either stagnated or declined. In the chart below, you can see overall location count for the company, including by vertical. This includes results through the first nine months of 2025 relative to the same time of 2024 as well.

We continued to see growth associated with America’s Best, with an increase in store count to 1,049 locations. That's up from the 1,019 locations that the company had a year earlier. Eyeglass World, meanwhile, has seen a continual decline in location count since 2023, while the other segments for the company have either flatlined, or in the case of its Fred Meyer locations, dropped lately.

Profitability for the company has been rather mixed, as one of the previous charts illustrates. But the good news is that, for 2025, things are looking up. As the chart above illustrates, revenue expanded to $1.48 billion during the first nine months of 2025. That's up from the $1.39 billion reported the previous year. Overall profitability surged, while cash flows rose nicely. During this period, the company benefited significantly from comparable store sales growth. In fact, when it came to the business as a whole, this was 5.7%, with America’s Best rising a hefty 6.7% year over year.

Management attributed this to higher average ticket prices at a time when customer traffic was essentially flat. This is interesting to me, but not necessarily surprising. When you are one of the cheapest players out there, or perhaps the cheapest, and you operate in a space where what you provide is essentially needed by a large chunk of the population, you can hike prices so long as your price is still comparable to or lower than the competition. This would obviously have a huge positive impact on profitability as well, which is precisely what we have seen.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management projected revenue of between $1.97 billion and $1.99 billion. At the midpoint, we would be looking at revenue of $1.98 billion, which would represent an 8.5% increase year over year. The firm is on track, based on the results achieved for the first nine months of the year, to generate EBITDA of around $182.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $148.3 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved in 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently priced. These are not bad multiples by any means, but they do put the business well in the fair value category as I describe it. They are certainly more expensive than they were when I last wrote about the business. In that chart, however, I did show that, compared to competitor Warby Parker (WRBY), which is admittedly not the perfect comparable given its e-commerce-oriented business model and its partnership with Google parent Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) aimed at providing smart glasses, shares are considerably cheaper. But I do chalk that up more to business model disparities than an indicator of actual value that investors can capitalize on.

Long term, we should expect continued growth from the company. For years, management had been growing at a rate of about 60 locations per year. This occurred between 2019 and 2023. But for the 2024 through 2027 years, it has been targeting slower pace of growth, with an average of about 30 stores per year. However, between the investments the company is making in innovation, changes in its competitive position through pricing, and the like, it expects to resume the more accelerated store growth path of 60 locations per year between 2028 and 2030. The fact that management has confidence in this is certainly encouraging, though we will have to wait and see if this expansion materializes.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I would argue that National Vision Holdings is an interesting prospect. But it's not one that I find to be the most compelling. The company should continue to grow nicely, though shares are not as cheap as I would like them to be. It is in light of this valuation that, even though I like the business from a growth perspective, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes sense.

data.title: SLJY: 18% Target Income Silver Covered Call ETF To Buy
data.description:
The Amplify SILJ Covered Call ETF (SLJY) is a relatively new (established August 2025) covered call ETF with a focus on silver, or, more specifically, junior silver mining companies, which exhibit a fairly strong correlation with silver (SLV) itself.

As most Amplify ETF products, SLJY is also rather unique, embodying certain properties that are not that widely used among other well-established covered call ETF names such as the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ).

The same could be said if we compare SLJY to some other silver covered call ETF alternatives: the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO) and the Kurv Silver Enhanced Income ETF (KSLV).

If we put this on a chart - comparing SLJY to its closest peers, silver and silver miners (SIL) - we will immediately arrive at some fundamental observations:

First, silver and silver miners are strongly correlated (i.e., experience the same rate of change patterns), but the magnitude of these movements can differ significantly.

Second, even though SIL has surged and SLJY carries a covered call factor, the realized returns have been almost identical, which sends a message that the embedded opportunity cost that sits within SLJY's covered call wrapper is fairly limited.

I think that this is the right segway to transition the next chapter in which I elaborate more on SLJY's offering and how (why) it could be used for strategic portfolio income enhancement.

Thesis

Let's start with the big picture.

As the chart below shows, silver miners and silver are correlated, but with certain periods in which more pronounced divergences can happen:

All in all, both of these asset classes are subject to the same demand drivers:

  • Technology and AI (electronic components for data centers).
  • Inflation protection and shield against dollar decline (store of value).
  • Demand from China.
  • Solar energy and EVs (critical component to enable these assets).

However, there are some structural differences. As opposed to pure-play silver holding, silver miners come with equity risk, which automatically brings to the equation such variables as financial risk (balance sheet management), execution risk and the ability to convert the current megatrend into real/tangible cash flows. Plus, an argument could be made that equity holdings are not as solid of a hedge against dollar depreciation and inflation as the physical metal.

Having said that, in the long-run these two assets tend to mean-revert, which given the current situation provides an opportunity to transform the disconnect into a real and potentially lucrative investment play - i.e., buying silver miners and expecting an outperformance from the mean-reversion process.

Unless one has a very high conviction view on physical silver and it is a bullish one, then I don't see a meaningful counterargument to a silver miner play. As for a value and income investor, the current divergence between the two asset groups actually renders SIL more interesting for me to consider integrating in the portfolio.

Now, and here comes SLJY's part and why it is, in my view, a very solid vehicle to implement that trade in an income-friendly (actually, income rich) manner.

As I briefly mentioned in the intro section, SLJY is a covered call ETF, which carries silver miners in its underlying portfolio. What it means is that SLJY invests directly in silver miners (has around 60 holdings, including calls) and then sells call options against these individual names (not on an index level).

The individual stock covered call writing generally allows it to capture higher volatility (and thus higher option income premium) relative to an index-level overwrite strategy. FEPI is a great example of that, offering a yield of around 25%, while comparable AI-focused covered call ETFs yield between 12% to 15%.

Speaking of SLJY's covered calls, it is also important to appreciate that we are talking about out-of-the-money contracts, which leave decent upside potential in the system. This is also the main reason SLJY has not significantly underperformed the booming SIL index. Usually, the key disadvantage of covered call strategies is that they cap the upside, which in the upwards trending markets can introduce a sizeable opportunity cost (i.e., not being able to profit from the share/asset price appreciation).

For example, one of the largest option contracts that SLJY currently has in its portfolio is related to its Hecla Mining (HL) stake. The call is almost 20% out of the money, which leaves ample upside for SLJY to participate in.

On top of this well-managed opportunity cost (or risk), SLJY has a strategy in place that aims to achieve an annualized option income of 18%:

By incorporating a tactical OTM covered call options writing strategy, the fund seeks to monetize volatility to generate 18% annualized option premium income while also offering the upside potential of junior silver mining companies

It means that SLJY calibrates an option overwrite portion and the level of out of the moneyness so that it can capture 18% of option income. If we annualize the two most recent distributions, then we would get a forward or implied dividend yield of ~15%. It is a bit lower than the stated target, but given the increasing distribution trajectory and SLJY active management (e.g., making decisions upon call option contract expiries how far to go in the OTM and overwrite areas), it would be only reasonable to assume that on a go forward basis the amount of income collected from SLJY should correspond to the ~18% target level.

So far, the story sounds quite attractive:

  • Participating in a silver bull run.
  • Opening an investment play with a silver miner vs. physical silver mean-reversion.
  • Collecting 18% in passive income streams.

The thing that could turn the entire case upside down is obviously a major correction, which is not atypical in the commodity and miner space.

If the silver market plunges, the chances will be high that SIL and SLJY would follow in the same direction. While SLJY would be better protected than the index due to the collected option premiums, but that would not be enough to avoid the risk of locking lower option contract values on a depreciated asset base. In that moment, the annual income stream level would likely remain the same (18% of the asset base), but the absolute level of income would come in much lower simply because of the smaller denominators (i.e., lower portfolio value).

The bottom line

SLJY presents an attractive opportunity for income investors to integrate silver-linked exposure into their portfolios, while still ticking the "yield-bearing asset" box, which would not be possible in a pure-play silver investment case or even if that trade was expressed via the silver miner index, which yields less than 1%.

The 18% income component that is fueled by OTM option income makes SLJY even more appealing - a real weapon that can enhance the overall portfolio income.

SLJY is certainly a covered call ETF to consider for income investors, who are bullish on silver and can stomach potentially violent downside volatility, which tends to happen in the commodity space. The downside volaltility would not only affect the price level, but also decrease the income stream accordingly.

However, if consistent and stable passive income is a factor that cannot be sacrificed, then SLJY might not be the right choice.

data.title: Madison Mid Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
For the full year, the Russell Midcap Index returned 10.6%, capping off an exceptionally strong three year run, amounting to an annual increase of over 14%. Similar to recent years, in 2025, market leadership favored more volatile and speculative companies. This was most pronounced in the strong rebound off of the Russell Midcap Index’s low on April 8th, from which areas like unprofitable technology companies, bitcoin exposed equities, and quantum computing businesses advanced over 100%. There were also several other more speculative areas of the market that advanced meaningfully during the year. This type of market environment typically represents a headwind for our portfolio, given our focus on high-quality, highly profitable, durable businesses, which we believe sell for reasonable prices. Historically, this has been a strong foundation for attractive long-term investment performance, and we remain committed to this discipline. In fact, we’re excited by the opportunities that the market presented in our types of businesses during the year and we were active in taking advantage of them.

Portfolio Performance

The Madison Mid Cap Fund (GTSGX) decreased 1.2% in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared to the 0.2% increase in the Russell Midcap Index.

The top five contributors for the quarter were Ross Stores (ROST), MKS (MKSI), Waters (WAT), PACCAR (PCAR), and Amphenol (APH). Ross Stores reported very strong sales results in the quarter and management offered an optimistic outlook for the rest of the year. The company is benefiting from its strategy to offer even better value, by further emphasizing familiar brands at low prices. Sales are up across all categories and more consumers are visiting Ross stores. MKS shares advanced nicely during the quarter as the business continues to benefit from end markets picking up. Specifically, their Electronics and Packaging business is showing accelerating growth, partly due to AI-related applications. Waters’ 3Q results reflected continued strong organic growth in both its instruments and consumables businesses as well as across all geographies.

PACCAR’s current results continued to be pressured by the depressed end market environment, but tariff relief, environmental regulations, and a cyclical end market recovery point to results improving from here. Amphenol’s IT/Datacom business continues to produce spectacular organic growth on the back of Al-related data center spending, which is driving up the earnings and share price of the stock.

The bottom five detractors for the quarter were Trex (TREX), Brown & Brown (BRO), CDW (CDW), Floor & Décor (FND), and Liberty Broadband (LBRDA). Trex shares declined meaningfully following their 3Q results and management’s outlook for 4Q. The company is facing headwinds from a slowing demand environment as well as an intensifying competitive environment. Brown & Brown reported another quarter of slowing organic growth and management suggested the fourth quarter will see more of the same. While growth is slowing, it is simply moderating to a more normalized level and profitability remains high, setting up the company well to continue to compound value over the long run. CDW reported improving revenue growth in 3Q, however, operating expenses grew faster in the quarter, so earnings growth was muted. Longer term, we believe that expenses will be held in check and earnings growth will pick back up.

Floor & Décor lagged the market as the difficult housing market continues to weigh on the sales of flooring. We believe the company is managing well through this cycle. Liberty Broadband, which derives nearly all its value from its ownership stake in Charter Communications, dropped as Charter reported a second straight quarter of disappointing profitability and broadband subscriber losses. Despite this, we believe the prospects for increased free cash flow provide solid valuation support for the stock.

Portfolio Activity

We added four new investments to the portfolio: Bio-Techne, ServiceTitan, A.O. Smith, and MSA Safety. Bio-Techne (TECH) is a leader in the life sciences research market with a broad portfolio of products that are used to enable discoveries of new drugs, therapeutics, and diagnostics. These products include over 6,000 proteins, 400,000 antibody types, and 2,400 diagnostic assays. Around 80% of the business is recurring revenues. We are encouraged by the new CEO’s strategy to leverage the core business and expand the company’s leadership position in protein research. Consternation surrounding the health of customer research spending gave us the opportunity to buy the stock at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

ServiceTitan (TTAN) is the dominant provider of software to residential trade contractors, such as HVAC technicians, plumbers, and pest control employees. Its software helps run all aspects of their business, including dispatching, marketing, invoicing, and estimating, and the company boasts high retention rates and strong recurring revenue. We see a long runway of growth ahead as penetration among contractors has lagged other industries. Investor caution regarding near-term growth rates provided us with a buying opportunity.

A.O. Smith (AOS) designs and manufactures water heaters, boilers, and water treatment products. Around 80-85% of water heater demand is from replacement units, providing for a more consistent demand profile. A.O. Smith is the market share leader, operating within a rational oligopoly that drives high returns on invested capital. The company is well positioned to benefit from upcoming energy regulations as well as improved capital deployment from new CEO Steve Shafer. The stock traded down to an appealing valuation following weakening results in its China business as well as general negative sentiment on anything housing-related.

MSA Safety (MSA) is the dominant provider of fire fighter safety equipment, gas/flame detection equipment, and industrial protective products. The company benefits from leading market positions and strong brand recognition. Growth is driven by technological advancements and updated regulatory standards that catalyze multi-year replacement cycles. The business is conservatively managed, has low balance sheet leverage, and generates fantastic returns on invested capital. The stock is trading at an attractive valuation given the quality of the business and what we believe is the potential for growth to accelerate.

We sold two holdings, Trex (TREX) and Microchip Technology (MCHP). Shortly after we purchased Trex, their largest competitor, Azek, was acquired by a large building products company, James Hardie. Hardie offered Azek greater scale for marketing and promoting their brands, and had a reputation for being very aggressive in going after market share and distribution agreements. Trex’s third quarter earnings report suggested that margins would be lower next year as they fend off this competitive threat. Additionally, demand has started to slow, providing another headwind to margins. We concluded that the competitive environment had meaningfully changed since our purchase and that Trex’s margins and earnings power may be lower for a prolonged period, thus we decided to sell.

Microchip Technology is one of the leading analog and microcontroller companies, benefiting from long product lifecycles and diverse end markets. Microchip struggled with supply chain challenges over the past 24 months as lengthening production lead times led to customer over-ordering. The company’s founder, Steve Sanghi returned to the CEO role this past summer and has been successful in reducing excess inventory and positioning the company for a recovery in market share. We are encouraged by Steve’s action plans, but as the stock’s valuation has recovered, we elected to sell our small stake.

We trimmed our position in Amphenol (APH) during the quarter, on valuation. We brought the position size down to a level commensurate with the stock’s current risk/reward following a tremendous run of strong performance for the business and the stock.

Outlook and Positioning

As always, we continue our search for quality companies with shareholder-oriented management teams selling below our assessments of intrinsic value. We are invested in a variety of high-quality, durable businesses in a wide range of industries, selling for reasonable, or even cheap, prices. This has historically been a formula for solid, long-term performance. We don’t think that has changed. But recently, these attributes have been unpopular, as the market has favored the short-term over the long-term, the promise versus the proven, and momentum over durability. While we dedicate a considerable amount of time to understanding new technologies and using them ourselves we will not risk our investors’ hard-earned savings by abandoning a philosophy that makes rational, clear-headed sense to us, and that has worked for decades, in order to conform to something that may be fleeting, and appears to be dependent on highly optimistic scenarios. Our style of companies may temporarily be out of favor, but we believe our all-weather portfolio remains well-positioned to deliver attractive risk adjusted returns in a variety of market environments.

Respectfully,

Haruki Toyama

Andy Romanowich

Rich Eisinger
