Portfolio Turnover: a measure of the trading activity in an investment portfolio—how often securities are bought and sold by a portfolio. The range represents the typical turnover of the portfolio.

Wtd Avg. Market Cap: the size of the companies in which the fund invests. Market capitalization is calculated by number of a company’s shares outstanding times its price per share.

Standard Deviation: a statistical measurement of dispersion about an average, which, for a portfolio, depicts how widely the returns varied over a certain period of time. Investors may use the standard deviation of historical performance to understand the range of returns for a portfolio. When a portfolio has a higher standard deviation than its benchmark, it implies higher relative volatility. Standard deviation has been calculated using the trailing monthly total returns for the appropriate time period. The standard deviation values are annualized.

Upside Capture Ratio: a fund’s performance in up markets relative to its benchmark. The security’s upside capture return is divided by the benchmark’s upside capture return over the time period.

Downside Capture Ratio: a fund’s performance in down markets relative to its benchmark. The security’s downside capture return is divided by the benchmark’s downside capture return over the time period.

Beta: a measure of the fund’s sensitivity to market movements. A portfolio with a beta greater than 1 is more volatile than the market, and a portfolio with a beta less than 1 is less volatile than the market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Banco Santander: I Am Cautious As The Long-Term Risk-Reward Proposition Is Not Great
data.description:
As I've covered back in October, I wasn't particularly bullish on Banco Santander, S.A. (SAN) as the bank’s premium valuation compared to historical values and its relatively weak earnings momentum were two major factors making me cautious. Despite that, its shares have performed quite well since then and have maintained their bull run, being up by close to 28% when including dividends since then and beating the S&P 500 Index (SP500) by a wide margin during the same period.

This strong performance is mainly justified by a higher valuation multiple rather than earnings or book value growth, as the market continues to push banking valuations upwards, supported by good capital return prospects for shareholders.

Banco Santander has been no exception and is now trading at about 1.62x book value, a much higher valuation than when I last covered it (1.27x book value at the time), despite its earnings momentum being somewhat weak and its growth prospects not being particularly impressive. As shown in the next graph, Santander’s current valuation is at its peak over the last decade and has re-rated considerably in recent months, a trend that has been widespread across the European banking sector.

Despite higher valuations and positive investor sentiment toward European banks, not all banks are reporting higher earnings in recent quarters as the interest rate path has been downward in Europe since mid-2024. As Southern European banks are more exposed to rates than other European banks due to higher exposure to variable-rate loans, the earnings momentum of Banco Santander and some of its peers has not been particularly great in recent quarters, mainly due to some margin pressure in its domestic market. Indeed, the bank’s earnings on Q3 2025 were practically flat when adjusting for the hyperinflation in Argentina, not being supportive of a higher share price lately.

This relatively weak earnings momentum is also expected to have been maintained during the fourth quarter of 2025 (with earnings expected to be released in the coming days, on or about February 4th), considering that according to analysts’ estimates Santander’s revenues over the full year are expected to be around €62 billion, being slightly down compared to the previous year. Its operating earnings should increase by less than 1%, as the bank’s cost-cutting program resulted in some operating efficiencies, while the bottom-line is expected to be around €13.4 billion, up by mid-single digits compared to 2024. However, investors should note that most gains are related to the Argentina hyperinflation adjustment, while adjusted net earnings should be rather flat compared to the previous year.

For 2026, the street also seems to be somewhat pessimistic about the bank’s earnings momentum given that revenues are expected to decline a little bit to below €61.8 billion and its operating earnings should decline by 1% YoY to €35.9 billion. This shows that Santander’s business model is quite sensitive to interest rates as the vast majority of revenues are generated from net interest income and the business is still quite exposed to variable-rate loans.

This profile is not expected to change much in the near future as Santander’s business model is highly reliant on retail and commercial loans, even though new loans production has a more balanced mix between fixed and variable rates. Additionally, while other European banks have used their securities' portfolio to hedge some interest rate sensitivity to rates, as I’ve recently covered on Barclays (BCS) for instance, Santander’s bond portfolio only represents 8% of its total assets and is not a major contributor to its overall net interest income. This is another reason why Santander’s revenues have been more sensitive to rate movements in Europe than some of its peers, a trend that is likely to persist ahead.

On the other hand, the European Central Bank has stopped cutting rates back in mid-2025, which means the bank’s NII is likely to stabilize in the second half of 2026 and may, eventually, start to increase thereafter if Santander pushes for loan growth. However, this has not happened during the first nine months of 2025, as the loan book was flat at €597 billion, plus the bank has two pending deals to sell its Polish operations and buy TSB Bank from Banco Sabadell (BNDSY) in England, which should have a slightly negative effect on NII.

This means Santander’s growth prospects over the next few quarters aren’t particularly impressive, but despite that, its shares have performed quite well over the past few months, as I’ve discussed previously, as investors seem to be more focused on the bank’s capital return prospects.

This happens because Santander’s capital ratio was 13.1% at the end of last September, which is already above its internal target range of being between 12-13% over the medium term, while the sale of 49% of the Polish unit will generate additional capital around €6.4 billion and have a positive effect on Santander’s CET1 ratio of about 100 basis points. While a good part of this capital generation will be used to acquire TSB in England (around €3 billion), this still leaves plenty of excess capital to perform further share buybacks and grow its ordinary dividend ahead.

The bank’s guidance is to perform at least some €10 billion of share buybacks between 2025-26, but I think this goal is likely to be revised upwards over the coming months when Santander finalizes its M&A deals, being an important support for its share price. Given that Santander’s current market value is about €160 billion, this share buyback program represents some 6.3% of its current market value, with a larger part expected to be performed during 2026 as the bank should have done only around €3.3 billion of share buybacks during 2025. This means Santander will be more active on the capital markets during this year repurchasing its own shares, which is an important support for its share price ahead.

On top of share buybacks, the bank also makes frequent dividend distributions, with the total dividend related to 2025 earnings expected to be around €0.23 per share. This is slightly below the market estimate when I last covered Santander, as the bank’s dividend policy is directly related to earnings and there have been some downward earnings revisions recently. At its current share price, this leads to a forward dividend yield about 2.16%, which is much lower than in the recent past due to a strong share price rally and is not particularly attractive to income-oriented investors.

In my opinion, this is also a sign of some overvaluation as there are plays in the European banking sector offering much higher dividend yields and the average dividend yield across European banks is about 3.8%. While in the past Santander was an attractive income pick and offered dividend yields consistently between 4-5%, this is no longer the case as the share price increases have clearly outpaced earnings and dividend growth in recent years.

Conclusion

While I see Banco Santander as a quality bank in the European banking sector and its exposure to emerging markets usually give it better growth prospects than compared to peers, this has changed over the past few quarters due to a declining interest rate environment in Europe. Santander’s business model is highly sensitive to rates and the bank’s asset mix doesn’t help much to offset sensitivity to rates, like some of its competitors have done recently to hedge NII against falling rates. Not surprisingly, Santander’s earnings momentum has been relatively weak recently, and this profile is not expected to change much during 2026.

Despite this background, its shares continued a strong share price rally in recent months, as the market seems to be more focused on capital returns than earnings growth. This has pushed its valuation to levels not seen over the past decade, which doesn’t seem to be sustainable in the near future. While I’m not expecting its share price to be weaker ahead if the equity bull run continues over the next few months, I think the current risk-reward proposition is not particularly attractive to long-term investors. Moreover, from an income perspective its appeal is also low and, therefore, I remain cautious about SAN shares for the time being.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VYM: Balanced Fundamentals And A Moderate Yield Is What Attracts $71B In AUM
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), a $71B product that's performed well this year relative to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Though many analysts have declared a sentiment shift that favors the smaller, more value-oriented stocks VYM holds, this article argues that this declaration is premature and that it's not yet reasonable to expect VYM to outperform SPY. However, this article also evaluates VYM against four large-cap value peers, illustrating how its balance, both at the sector and fundamental levels, is why it's historically been a consistent performer year over year. Overall, these features make VYM a solid "hold" for investors seeking a moderate ~2.50% dividend yield, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below. I hope you enjoy the read.

VYM Overview

One challenge with assessing VYM is that its strategy is proprietary. An Index methodology document exists, but it offers few clues surrounding the selection process beyond the basics. These include how the selection universe for the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index is the FTSE US All Cap Index, how its membership is reviewed annually, and how REITs and non-dividend-paying securities are not eligible. VYM's cheap 0.06% expense ratio and 0.01% 30-day median bid/ask spread are other noteworthy factors, but in my opinion, they don't really help us differentiate it from the 100+ other competing funds.

However, we can learn more about what the Index likely aims to accomplish by evaluating it fundamentally. To start, consider how the dividend yields of VYM's top holdings are not in descending order, with Broadcom's (AVGO) 0.81% dividend yield in the #1 spot and higher-yielding stocks like Chevron (CVX) and Philip Morris International (PM) in the #13 and #19 spots, respectively. Instead, the correlation between weight and market cap appears much stronger, which tells me that weight is likely a function of a company's size, yield, and the Index possibly considers secondary factors like liquidity and sector mix.

One reason to temper expectations about VYM's performance is because it's historically delivered about average returns. As shown below, VYM's annual returns ranked in the second or third quartile in most years between 2016 and 2025. There were some exceptions, like in 2022, when VYM's exposure to Energy stocks proved beneficial in the high-inflation environment. However, that benefit quickly reversed the following year, when inflation peaked, and growth-oriented funds started to perform better. While traders may capitalize on these short-term movements, I believe VYM's appeal comes mainly from delivering consistent, slightly better-than-average returns most of the time.

VYM Performance Summary

While SPY mostly reflects VYM's selection universe, it's not an appropriate comparator. For example, I estimate its indicated yield is more than 2x higher (2.54% vs. 1.17%, excluding fund expenses), and its $318B weighted average market cap is about $1T lower. After applying some reasonable constraints (market cap between $100-$500B, estimated yield above 2%, 10+ year track record), I found only 15 peers. Their performance statistics from January 2016 to December 2025 are summarized below, and they show how VYM's 11.32% annualized return ranks #7/16 in this group.

VYM shareholders will also appreciate how its 23.98% maximum drawdown in Q1 2020 wasn't unusual at all. In contrast, the top-performing WisdomTree U.S. Value ETF (WTV) declined by 31.43% that quarter, and its performance statistics are obviously skewed by its 77.27% "best twelve months" result in the year that followed. To avoid performance chasing, I strongly recommend evaluating WTV based on its fundamentals, and although it's not the subject of today's article, I can say that it scores lower than VYM in my fundamentals-driven rankings system as of January 24, 2026.

Still, it's hard to ignore the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), the Vanguard Mega Cap Value ETF (MGV), and the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV). All three were better than VYM on annualized returns, maximum drawdowns, worst twelve months, and risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios), and I find the differences in estimated yields to be negligible (~0.40%). For those not wanting to compromise yield, I will also look at the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) with the understanding that its Index is scheduled to reconstitute on March 23, 2026. As discussed here, I anticipate a more fundamentally balanced portfolio will emerge on that date, which should hopefully get it back on track from a total returns perspective this year.

VYM Analysis

VYM Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

A key reason why VYM delivers consistently solid annual returns is because it's well-balanced at the sector level. As shown below, Financials is its largest sector at 22.27%, but it also has 15.56% allocated to Technology, and that helps ensure at least reasonable participation in bull markets. In contrast, SCHD's Index is lighter on Technology (8%) and heavier on Energy (21%) and Consumer Staples (18%), and that combination hasn't worked well in the strong bull market from 2023 to 2025.

Though VYM's Index is subject to change at each reconstitution, its portfolio turnover rate ranged from just 8-13% for the last five fiscal years. When you think about all that's transpired over this period, these rates are incredibly low and suggest the Index is designed not to panic. In my view, this contributes to its consistent returns profile, whereas higher turnover ETFs like SCHD have much more sensitive inputs and, thus, are expected to either substantially outperform or underperform in any given year. That's why I place so much emphasis on SCHD's annual reconstitutions and not so much on VYM's.

VYM Fundamental Analysis: Top 20 Holdings

The following table highlights selected fundamental statistics for VYM's top 20 holdings, which comprise 40.09% of its portfolio. What's interesting is that these statistics do not definitively conclude that VYM is better than DGRO, MGV, or VTV in its current state. It depends on what factors you value, so let's go through them one by one to understand the tradeoffs.

Market Risk

VYM's five-year portfolio beta is 0.83, which is almost identical to DGRO, MGV, and VTV. Further down the market risk line is SCHD at 0.66, while SPY's is substantially higher at 1.14. You're probably used to seeing SPY's beta pegged to 1.00, which is standard practice for data providers. However, this weighted average calculation illustrates just how much riskier core funds have become as high-risk stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), with a five-year beta of 2.31, have cemented their leadership. This fact alone may be reason enough for some investors to reduce their risk, and large-cap value ETFs in general are a good way to do so.

Growth

VYM's components have increased their sales and earnings per share by about 5% per year over the last three years, with analysts expecting 9.77% more earnings growth over the next twelve months. DGRO, MGV, and VTV have similar growth rates, which is why they're reasonable alternatives, while SCHD's components actually saw their earnings decline by an annualized 7.38% over the last three years. Weak or negative growth is a persistent problem for SCHD lately, so it's no surprise to see its returns were only 4.20% annualized from 2023 to 2025, with nearly all of this sourced to dividends rather than share price appreciation.

VYM's growth rates are competitive within this peer group, but we shouldn't ignore how far apart they are from SPY's. Since SPY's components increased EPS by 3x more over the last three years (15.27% vs. 4.95%), it's important to call out the potential for a significant tradeoff on total returns. Consider that even if the market's lofty growth expectations aren't met, VYM will need a meaningful valuation compression, likely in the tech sector, to close the gap and start outperforming SPY. DGRO, MGV, and VTV face a similar challenge.

Valuation

VYM currently trades at 20.49x trailing earnings and 17.27x forward earnings based on data from January 24, 2026. That's about 1-2 points cheaper than DGRO and MGV, on par with VTV, and about two points more than SCHD. This demonstrates the kind of balance VYM achieves and what so many investors appreciate, even if they aren't tuned in to these specifics. It's obviously not the cheapest fund in its category, but when factoring in its growth rates, it's arguably the best of the five.

Dividends

VYM's turnover rates are low, which means that most of its reported dividend growth relates to the dividend growth rates of its underlying holdings. In contrast, ETFs like SCHD can synthetically create more dividend growth by systematically rebalancing into high-yielding stocks. From a DGI perspective, this is a smart way to take advantage of an ETF's open-ended structure, which allows for "in-kind" transactions to occur with no tax consequences. However, the downside is that it could mask real fundamental issues with an ETF's underlying holdings. For example, the earnings for SCHD's holdings decreased by an annualized 7.38% over the last three years, yet those same holdings increased their dividends by an annualized 6.76%. It doesn't take much of an analyst to realize that this gap is unsustainable - the expected behavior is for markets to limit share price appreciation, which is exactly what happened with SCHD.

Fortunately, I believe VYM is on solid ground when it comes to dividend yield, dividend consistency, dividend safety, and dividend growth. As shown below, its 2.48% estimated yield (2.54% Index yield less 0.06% expense ratio) is a bit better than average, its 4.95% three-year historical EPS growth rate supports adequate dividend growth without stretching the payout ratio too much, and the ETF itself is on an impressive 15-year dividend growth streak.

That long streak itself doesn't necessarily mean dividend growth is key to the selection process. Keep in mind that SPY is also on a 16-year dividend growth streak, and all it represents is 500 of the largest U.S. stocks by market cap. Instead, the likely reason for the streak is that its holdings are on good financial footing, and in its own way, VYM does that reasonably well.

Quality

We can measure if an ETF is on good financial footing by calculating the same profitability statistics you've probably come across using stock screeners. All that's required is to merge stock data and fund weightings while applying some soft constraints to limit the influence of outliers, and after doing this, I found that VYM ranks about average on several measures compared to its large-cap value ETF peers, including:

  • Asset Turnover: 0.68 (#86/149)
  • EBIT Margin: 22.59% (#65/149)
  • Net Margin: 17.83% (#52/149)
  • ROA: 6.83% (#94/149)
  • ROE: 21.97% (#68/149)
  • ROTC: 11.17% (#85/149)

There's no universal measure of quality, but VYM scoring close to average on numerous profitability measures aligns well with what we've observed with its performance. My ranking system, which aggregates these statistics while emphasizing operational efficiency (EBIT margins) and capital efficiency (return on total capital), suggests DGRO is a bit better on quality. However, SPY's 8.48/10 quality composite score is miles ahead of all five large-cap value funds and reminds us that the largest companies also happen to be the most profitable. As a result, I don't advise betting against them.

Momentum and Sentiment

I mentioned earlier how large-cap value ETFs have done well so far this year. As shown below, VYM is up 3.55%, while SCHD, the most value-oriented fund discussed today, is up 7.11%. Given how SPY's gain is rather modest at 1.99%, many are wondering whether the tide has finally shifted away from growth and into value.

I'm encouraged by these results, but I also remain skeptical, mainly because SPY's holdings are still delivering solid earnings surprises. My S&P 500 Index EPS Surprise Tracker notes how the Index is on an impressive 11-quarter streak of above-average earnings surprises, and the Index's weighted surprise last quarter was close to 9% compared to about 6% for VYM. To be sure, sentiment can change quickly, but I prefer to wait for hard numbers to come in this quarter before assessing if value stocks really are ready to take over.

Investment Recommendation

I think VYM is in pretty good shape fundamentally. Its estimated dividend yield is 2.48%, which is about 0.40% more than close peers like DGRO, MGV, and VTV. While this yield is well below that of SCHD's, the upside is a stronger earnings growth rate that supports sustained and organic dividend growth and a better-balanced portfolio all around, both from a fundamental and sector allocation perspective. This balance is why VYM consistently delivers close-to-average returns each year compared to its category peers, and that's the most important thing readers should know about the fund.

Over the long term, I prefer higher quality over cheaper valuations, so I lean towards DGRO over VYM, and I think that's supported by its superior total and risk-adjusted returns. However, VYM is still a good choice for those aiming for consistency over trying to beat the market using a more active approach, so for those reasons, it deserves a solid "hold" rating. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: SP500: Sell The Highs, All Risk Events Delayed To February
data.description:
The All-time high resistance

The S&P 500 (SP500) is at the all-time high resistance that has been building since October 2025 - so that's four months of flat performance. In my opinion, this reflects a topping pattern. The price tried to breach the resistance on several occasions, unsuccessfully.

The key support level is the rising 100dma (red line). Similarly, there were several attempts to breach the support level, also unsuccessfully. The 100dma support is also the bottom of the rising regression channel since the April 2025 bottom.

Thus, we have a narrowing pattern or flag where the S&P 500 is unable to break to the new highs, but at the same time, it's holding the uptrend support.

Obviously, this situation will resolve itself either with 1) the breakout to the new all-time highs to the 7500 level at the top of the regression channel or 2) the 100dma breakdown to the next support level at 6500 - which could mark the beginning of the major correction and possibly even a recessionary bear market with the bubble burst.

The breakdown is more likely

Technically, we can simply follow the chart and see what happens, where the proper strategy is to buy the breakout or sell the breakdown - no questions asked.

However, fundamentally, a breakdown is more likely.

First of all, we are not in a recession, although many soft-data indicators like consumer and business confidence are at recession levels. Yet, we are not trading based on recession fears at the moment.

Second, inflation is not a problem - officially. The data is pointing to modestly elevated inflation, which is slightly falling. However, unofficially, inflation is a major risk due to the currency debasement and Sell America themes, as we will explain in this article.

Third, credit risk is very low, based on very tight credit spreads - so this is also positive for the stock market.

Altogether, the macro situation appears to be very benign and supportive of the breakout scenario. However, the macro risks are rising, and it seems like most of these risks are converging over the next few weeks. Thus, fundamentally, the breakdown over the near term is more likely.

Given the benign macro environment and the fact that the S&P 500 is near the all-time highs, the VIX (VIX) should be very low in the 12-13 area. However, the VIX is at 16, which suggests that the macro risk is elevated.

The risk list

The risk list is getting longer by the day, and the probability of the negative outcome is very high for each risk - and these are all imminent events.

The Sell America trade - Geopolitics - Iran

Geopolitically, we are in the new world order, not simple de-globalization. De-globalization implies something controlled and peaceful. The formation of the new world order is something hostile. The Sell America trade is the outcome of this process, manifested with a falling US Dollar (DXY) and higher US yields (US10Y), and in some situations, higher oil prices (USO).

An imminent event is a likely US strike on Iran, which could happen imminently, as the US armada already arrived in the Gulf. This could cause the spike in the price of oil - and the increase in volatility is negative for the stock market.

In addition, the aftermath of the Greenland situation is likely to continue to support the Sell America trade, which is negative for the stock market.

An imminent government shutdown

The US government is likely to go into another shutdown after January 30th, as the Democrats in the Senate seem unlikely to support the funding for ICE after the unfortunate situation in Minneapolis over the weekend.

This even also supports a weakening US Dollar and higher yields, consistent with the Sell America trade.

The debasement trade - the new Fed Chair announcement

President Trump is also likely to announce the new Fed Chair imminently. If the new Fed Chair lacks market credibility, the market could also push the US Dollar lower and the US long-term rates higher due to the currency debasement fears - this is also consistent with the Sell America trade and negative for the stock market.

The SCOTUS decision on tariffs

The SCOUTS has delayed the decision on the legality of Trump tariffs to at least late February. Most expected the decision to come in January, but likely due to Trump's trip to Davos, the decision has been delayed.

If SCOTUS strikes down the Trump tariffs, the Trump economic plan essentially collapses, but Trump is likely to reimplement the tariffs using other statutes and possibly with other, more aggressive policies, which could accelerate the Sell America trade.

The unfolding Yen carry trade blow-up

Japanese JGB yields have been rising, and the Japanese Yen has started to spike, which means that this situation could cause the unwind of the yen carry trade or forced selling by the hedge funds who borrow in Yen to fund their positions.

In addition, Japanese investors could start selling US Treasuries to invest in the domestic bonds as the yield differential narrows, which could also result in higher US yields.

The potential AI bubble burst with the earnings season

The S&P 500 is a bubble trading at a Shiller PE ratio above 40 because the mega caps are overvalued due to the AI theme. The AI bubble could burst imminently during the current earnings season due to these issues: the questions about ROI in AI capex, the questions about AI chip depreciation, and the issue of debt financing and the circular investing themes. Even if the mega-caps beat the earnings estimates, any indication that these issues could become a problem could burst the bubble.

Implications

We are in a very unusual situation where many serious macro risks are converging over the next few weeks, while the S&P 500 is trading near the all-time high - at a bubble-like valuation.

It's always difficult to predict a market crash, but the current situation could produce one.

It seems like the channel to the stock market crash is through the depreciating US Dollar and higher US yields. The US dollar has started weakening in the aftermath of the Greenland situation. The 10Y yields also rose but retreated and remain stable - so this is the metric to follow.

The only indicator of the current situation is Gold (GLD) - an ultimate hedge against macro volatility related to a falling US Dollar, and the warning about the unfolding situation. Gold just breached the 5000 level.

Thus, in my opinion, the S&P 500 is likely to have a breakdown over the next few weeks as the situation unfolds, and my rating is a Strong Sell.

data.title: VGK: Europe Is Faced With Another Energy Crisis (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis.

The Vanguard FTSE Europe ETF (VGK) has had a good year in 2025. It comfortably outperformed the S&P 500. It helped to close the performance gap for the first half of this decade, despite a stagnated European economy. Part of the reason why Europe's economy has been mostly stagnated this decade is the high price of energy and overall energy insecurity issues.

Since the fall of 2024, it has become increasingly evident that there is a structural natural gas supply deficit in the European markets. We went from a sizable surplus above the five-year average in the early fall of 2024 to dipping below the five-year average at the start of 2025, and inventories are now 23% below the five-year average. Despite that, inexplicably, there has been no market reaction to the situation until the past week or so. The current

My prior coverage of VGK.

I last covered the VGK ETF at the start of December, and at that point, I was more optimistic about the prospects for an EU-Russia rapprochement that could help rebalance European energy markets and even bring prices down significantly, improving Europe's economic competitiveness. I figured then already that it must be what the markets are betting on, since there is no other explanation for the current market disconnect from the fundamentals.

I am moving from a hold to a sell because the weather has played a negative role in making Europe's natural gas inventory situation more dire than it was. At the same time, Russia seems less likely to agree to any kind of rapprochement without significant EU concessions on Russia's frozen assets and other matters. In the absence of such a rapprochement this year, the EU will enter next winter in dismal shape, unless it starts to aggressively bid up the price of natural gas to attract more supplies.

About the VGK ETF.

The VGK ETF offers broad exposure to the European corporate sector. It has a total of 1,234 holdings currently, versus the FTSE All Caps Europe Index that has 1,206. The performance of the ETF and the index is roughly similar over time.

The largest holdings in the ETF provide a glimpse into the fund's sectoral exposure, which is diverse, as well as a very important aspect that we need to pay attention to, namely, the level of dependence on the local market versus their international market exposure.

ASLM (ASML), for instance, sells most of its lithography machines in Asia.

As we can see, it has hardly any revenue exposure to the European market, which is the case to some degree or another with many other top holdings in the VGK fund. In other words, just because 99% of the holdings are European, it does not mean that exposure to the domestic European economy is anywhere near that level. Within the context of an impending energy crisis, the most positive aspect of this fund is arguably its international exposure.

Other notable figures for this ETF include a dividend of about 2.75%. The expense ratio is 0.06%. It trades at a P/E ratio of 17.2, versus the S&P 500 (SPY), which trades at 31. The comparatively low P/E ratio is what makes European markets and the VGK ETF arguably attractive, if one ignores other factors.

On the verge of another energy crisis.

One might not know it from looking at the behavior of the main European natural gas spot price market, but European storage levels have been declining relative to the five-year average since the fall of 2024.

Based on the projections I shared in the chart near the beginning of the article, we will dip below the technically challenging 1,000 Bcf level by the end of February or the start of March.

Beyond possible regional shortfalls that may occur this spring as a result of the current trajectory, unless European gas buyers start bidding up the price to attract more LNG supplies, the start of the 2026-2027 winter will leave Europeans in a very uncomfortable position. Inventory levels may be only about 50% full in September, as the chart shows. For comparison, inventories were far above 90% full in the fall of 2024 and 83% full in the fall of 2025.

The lack of market reaction to this situation is astonishing. It begs the question of whether or not the market may be expecting a sudden reversal in tensions with Russia this year that might lead to a return of Russian gas flows to Europe rather soon. Events on the ground do not seem to support that thesis. Even if there was a peace deal, the Russians are not likely to accept doing business with Europe unless issues such as Russia's central bank assets that were frozen are settled.

The US deep freeze may have amplified Europe's crisis.

US natural gas spot prices recently reached a three-year high.

The price of natural gas in the US is rising mostly due to the cold weather. Whether it will continue to rally will depend on the next few weekly storage reports. At the moment, US storage levels are adequate, but that could change fast, especially if the cold snap continues.

There are already reports of a reduction in US LNG exports, mostly due to weather-related logistical disruptions.

In a worst-case scenario for Europe, the Trump Administration may decide to curb US LNG exports to protect US consumers and the US economy more broadly from the inflationary risks associated with higher natural gas prices throughout the economy. Europe currently depends on US exports to cover about a fifth of its total natural gas demand.

EU decision to ban Russian natural gas imports in 2027.

Depending on weather conditions in the next two months or so, it is possible that Europe will avoid economically harmful shortfalls in natural gas supplies at the end of this winter. It may even be the case that just slightly higher natural gas prices in Europe will divert enough LNG exports from other markets to the European market to allow it to accumulate a decent volume of inventories for the next winter. The recent EU decision to completely ban Russian natural gas imports in 2027 will all but guarantee higher European natural gas prices beyond this year, regardless of the current situation and its outcome.

Russia still accounts for 13% of the EU's total natural gas supplies, or about 38 Bcm based on 2025 data.

It is unclear where Europe will source an additional 30-40 Bcm of natural gas annually to replace Russian gas supplies next year. US gas output is near stagnation, and Norway is on a gentle slope of output decline. Other major LNG exporters, such as Qatar, are mostly locked into long-term delivery contracts. In other words, even if by some miracle the European market avoids a natural gas price spike this year, next year's supply/demand fundamentals may turn out to be even more challenging.

Investment implications.

At the moment, it looks like market complacency in the past few months, with natural gas prices defying fundamentals, has had the net effect of aggravating the problem. The recent movement in the natural gas spot price suggests that the market is running out of room to continue to defy those fundamentals, but I could be wrong. We will likely see a European energy price crisis this year, which in turn will hurt the already struggling European economy, but it might still take some time for the fundamentals to become absolutely impossible to ignore.

As we can see, the VGK ETF is underperforming the SPY ETF (SPY), which tracks the performance of the S&P 500, on a longer time scale. Another shock to Europe's economy will impact many companies found in the VGK fund that do have significant exposure to their domestic market, ranging from BASF (BASFY) to Mercedes (MBGAF).

Judging by Europe's future natural gas supply growth prospects, especially within the context of the recent decision to finalize plans for completely banning Russian natural gas, even as US export volumes may fail to live up to expectations, the shortfall will continue beyond this year. Higher prices will attract more supplies. On the other side of the equation, it will trigger more demand destruction.

As the chart suggests, in the last few months of 2025, Europe's natural gas demand started to rebound after many years of steady decline. EU GDP growth rates averaged just over 1% in the 2020-2024 period. It looked like there would be an upshift in economic growth starting with last year, to about 1.5%/year.

Natural gas storage levels that have been steadily declining relative to the 5-year average for almost a year and a half now suggest that Europe simply does not have the energy needed for this level of economic expansion. We will therefore see a downward shift again. Within this context, the lower P/E ratio relative to the S&P 500 is not the bargain that one might assume it to be. Europe's domestic market is set to struggle for growth, while the international market can no longer be counted upon, since the post-WW2 World Order is starting to break down. This overall picture makes the VGK ETF a sell.

Risks to my thesis.

The news that the European Commission adopted a decision to eliminate Russian natural gas supplies from the market next year hardly suggests that a pivot back to importing large amounts of Russian natural gas is on the way. It would be perhaps the only solution to rectify the current disconnect between the market & the fundamentals that would not involve an energy price crisis. If such a pivot were to happen, it would be completely unexpected. We do live in an age when we should expect the unexpected; therefore, it should not be discounted as a possibility. It would explain why the European natural gas market is so calm in the face of a seemingly outright dangerous situation. Perhaps major traders know something that the broader public does not

Conclusions.

In an increasingly overpriced market, where the S&P 500 trades at double the P/E ratio relative to historical averages, I tend to look for investments that look underpriced. On the surface, the VGK ETF fits the profile, with a P/E ratio that is half that of the S&P 500. Once we take a closer look, the European domestic economy is feeble, with very low economic growth levels so far this decade. Another energy crisis, which is now looking all but inevitable based on the fundamentals, has the potential to throw the already struggling European economy into a tailspin. The VGK ETF is worth revisiting, perhaps once the energy situation plays out, but right now, the risk/reward factors I cited make it a low-potential upside, high-risk investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Weakens As Markets Reprice U.S. Political Risk
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

The dollar is being treated as a pressure-release mechanism for US debt — and markets are responding by demanding protection against intentional depreciation.

For decades, US dollar dominance rested on a simple but profound foundation. Predictable institutions made the dollar stable, on the belief — sometimes overstated — that the United States would not deliberately undermine its own currency. That belief is now visibly eroding.

The dollar has fallen to its weakest level in nearly four years, not because of a recession or crisis at home, but because investors are increasingly uneasy about the direction of American policy. Against a basket of other currencies, the US dollar is approaching the lows seen during the COVID pandemic as markets are beginning to price in something more corrosive than cyclical weakness. Political and institutional risk is emanating from Washington itself.

Bloomberg Dollar Index, 2020 – present

The immediate catalysts are not difficult to identify. A barrage of radical policy proposals — universal tariffs, explicit talk of engineering a weaker dollar to boost exports, revived speculation around a so-called Mar-a-Lago Accord, and even loose discussion of restructuring Treasury obligations — has injected deep uncertainty into currency markets. Add to that overt efforts to pressure the Federal Reserve toward lower interest rates, including attempts to shape the future composition of the FOMC, and the result is a growing conviction that the dollar is less insulated from political whim than at any point in recent history. Currency traders are responding accordingly. Options markets now show the most expensive hedges against dollar weakness since records began in 2011, while positioning across major currencies reflects a decisive shift away from the greenback.

What distinguishes this episode from earlier periods of dollar weakness is not simply the magnitude of the decline, but its character. Historically, the dollar tended to soften when global growth strengthened or when US monetary policy eased relative to its peers. Today, the US economy continues to perform reasonably well by conventional measures, yet the dollar is underperforming nearly every major peer currency. That disconnect is telling. Investors are no longer reacting solely to interest rate differentials or growth forecasts; they are embedding a political risk premium into the currency itself. Unpredictable Washington policymaking, threats against allies, widening fiscal deficits, and open speculation about currency coordination have transformed what was once a safe-haven asset into a policy-contingent one.

The renewed debate over coordinated foreign exchange intervention underscores that shift. Reports that US authorities have been checking dollar-yen levels, a step often associated with preparatory intervention, have revived memories of the 1985 Plaza Accord era, when the dollar was deliberately driven lower through multinational agreement. Whether or not any formal coordination ultimately emerges, the signal matters more than the mechanics. Markets interpret these gestures as tacit approval of dollar depreciation, particularly when paired with rhetoric favoring export competitiveness over currency stability. Once traders suspect policymakers are tolerant of a weaker currency, or actively seeking one, the long dollar trade becomes structurally fragile.

US Dollar Index versus gold price per ounce, Jan 2025 – present

This erosion of confidence is unfolding alongside a powerful and sustained rise in gold. Prices have surged above $5,000 an ounce after climbing roughly 85 percent over the past year. Silver, while more volatile and less purely monetary, has followed in its wake. These are not speculative curiosities; they are signals. Gold has long served as a barometer of trust in paper claims, especially when fiscal discipline and monetary independence come into question. That institutional investors, central banks, and sovereign wealth funds are among the largest buyers reinforces the point. This is not retail exuberance, but strategic reallocation.

The motivations behind this shift are straightforward. Large deficits, rising debt burdens, and persistent questions about the future independence of the Federal Reserve all raise doubts about the long-term purchasing power of dollar-denominated assets. When political actors treat interest rates, exchange rates, and even sovereign debt structure as tools to be manipulated for short-term advantage, investors naturally seek refuge in assets that lie outside the policy sphere altogether. Gold does not rely on promises, committees, or continuity of leadership. Its appeal rises precisely when those things appear uncertain.

This environment also helps explain the renewed seriousness of discussions around dedollarization. Contrary to some caricatures, dedollarization does not require the sudden collapse of the dollar or the emergence of a single rival currency. It is a gradual process of diversification: more trade invoiced in non-dollar currencies, more reserves held in gold or alternative assets, and more systematic hedging against dollar exposure. Recent strength in the euro, renewed interest in Asian currencies, and record highs in emerging market currency indices all point in this direction. When even long-standing US partners begin to question the durability of American policy commitments, diversification becomes a rational response rather than an ideological statement.

The rise in the dollar’s share of SWIFT transactions from roughly 38 percent five years ago to a little over 50 percent today does not, by itself, imply that the dollar is being adopted by more participants or that it has become structurally “stronger.” The SWIFT metric captures the share of transaction value denominated in a currency, not the number of users or the depth of confidence behind it, and that distinction is crucial. Over the past five years, higher US inflation has mechanically lifted nominal dollar transaction values even where real trade volumes have not increased, inflating the dollar’s apparent share without signaling greater monetary centrality.

At the same time, repeated episodes of geopolitical stress and financial volatility have driven derisking behavior, in which assets are liquidated, and capital is repatriated through dollar channels, temporarily boosting dollar settlement activity even as the longer-term appetite for dollar assets weakens. Legacy invoicing conventions in commodities, shipping, and trade finance also change slowly, meaning dollar usage can remain dominant or even rise in aggregate while marginal flows quietly diversify elsewhere. Taken together, the increase in SWIFT share over this period is better understood as a reflection of inflation, crisis-driven liquidity demand, and institutional inertia than as evidence of renewed confidence in the dollar’s long-run strength.

Real Trade-Weighted US Dollar and USD percent in SWIFT, Jan 2021 – present

Ironically, many of the policies intended to bolster US competitiveness may be accelerating this very shift. Tariffs invite retaliation and fragment trade relationships. Efforts to weaken the dollar to support exporters risk undermining confidence in US financial markets, which have long been among the country’s greatest competitive advantages. Pressure on the Federal Reserve blurs the line between monetary policy and politics, weakening the institutional credibility that supports low borrowing costs and anchors inflation expectations.

Markets, however, are rarely sentimental. They respond to incentives, signals, and risks as they appear, not as policymakers wish them to be interpreted. The dollar’s slide, the surge in gold, and the growing urgency of dedollarization discussions are all manifestations of the same underlying judgment: that the rules governing US economic policy are becoming less stable, less predictable, and more politicized.

Until that perception changes, skepticism toward the dollar and demand for monetary hedges are unlikely to fade.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Prosperity Bancshares: Efficient, And Growing, Buy The Dip
data.description:
We resume our regional bank coverage with the just-reported earnings out of Prosperity Bancshares, Inc. (PB). This bank has been growing organically and has been acquiring other banks. Earlier in 2025 they made a move to acquire American Bank in an all-stock deal valued at $322 million. Today we also learned that they are going to acquire Stellar Bancorp (STEL) in a cash and stock transaction valued at about $2 billion. This move will result in it being Texas's second largest bank by deposits.

However, as a result, PB shares are falling a bit on the news as this is no small acquisition to be sure. But long term, we continue to like the bank and reiterate a Buy rating. Let us discuss the results to add to the evidence base that regional banks are showing no signs of an economic slow down.

Prosperity Bancshares' Q4 Headline Performance

So far, regional banks have reported superb earnings in general. The Q4 revenues were $313.7 million, a 3.3% increase in this metric year-over-year. This was in line with consensus expectations. With a solid net interest margin and well controlled expenses and provisions for losses earnings were a $0.06 beat per share versus consensus. Net income hit $139.9 million, or $1.49 per share. A strong result. And, the book value expanded.

Book value per share was $81.84, so there is a sizable discount-to-book here at $67 per share at the time of this writing. It is a premium-to-tangible book however, as tangible book value came in at $43.64. Still, in the universe of our coverage, this is not expensive on this metric compared to many other regional banks. This is especially true as this is a serial dividend raiser, with dividend increases each year, and a yield well over 3%.

We reiterate a Buy. Let's talk loan and deposits.

Prosperity Bancshares' Q4 2025 Loans and Deposits

We of course follow trends in loans and deposits, as these are key metrics for any bank we look at. Well, a slight blemish here as loans declined from borrower prepayments. Not necessarily a negative. Loans at the end of Q4 were $22.03 billion, and this was a slight decrease from the sequential Q3 of about $222 million.

What about deposits? Well, deposits surged to $28.48 billion, up $0.7 billion from Q3. Nothing to worry about in either of these trends in our opinion.

Prosperity Bancshares' Q4 2025 Asset Quality and Return Metrics

We have noted that some banks have seen slight softening in their asset quality metrics. Prosperity has long enjoyed strong asset quality. Once again in Q4, there was no provision for credit losses at all. That is a positive result on its own. The allowance for credit losses on loans was $333.7 million, or 1.53% of total loans. This is down compared with $339.6 million in Q3, or 1.54% of all loans. That is also positive. We also saw very little net charge-offs. Net charge-offs were just $5.9 million, down from Q3's $6.5 million. One blemish on the quarter was a tick up in nonperforming assets. These increased to 0.69% of all loans from 0.54%, so we will continue to monitor that trend.

Turning to the return metrics, the bank still enjoys a 3% plus net interest margin, helping drive return metrics higher. The return on average assets expanded 5 basis points from 1.44% in Q3, to 1.49% here in Q4. This is strong. The return on average equity improved 12 basis points to 7.30% versus 7.18% in Q3. Finally, remains highly efficient. It is one of the most efficient banks we cover and in Q4, efficiency improved. The efficiency ratio improved from 44.06% in Q3, to 43.66% here in Q4. Efficiency has improved each quarter in 2025 as well.

Forward View

PB is a serial dividend raiser with strong key metrics. The asset quality metrics for the most part improved. Loans dipped slightly within standard variance while deposits surged. Book value is increasing and shares trade at a discount-to-book, with a moderate premium-to-tangible book. The bank is highly efficient and is acquiring banks. With its most recent deal with Stellar, it will grow to the second-largest bank by deposit base in Texas. Take advantage of the selloff.

data.title: Intel: Why A Turnaround Still Seems Unlikely
data.description:
Performance assessment

Intel Corporation (INTC) has beaten the market since my last Market Perform/Hold update on the stock:

Thesis

Intel reported Q4 FY25 earnings last week. Here's why I am not impressed:

  1. Revenue growth may be muted in the short to medium term
  2. Intel is still losing the market share battles.
  3. There are multiple gross margin headwinds.
  4. INTC is trading at a lower discount vs. peers.
  5. Bullish and bearish forces seem balanced.

In Aug'24, I had stated that a turnaround seems unlikely for Intel. I continue to believe that this is true today.

Revenue growth may be muted in the short to medium term

Intel is facing big supply constraints that is going to hinder revenue growth in H1 FY26:

As we enter 2026, our buffer inventory is depleted and the mix shift in wafers towards servers, which began in Q3 will not come out of fab until late Q1 '26. As a result, and as we stated last quarter, our internal supply constraints are most acute in Q1.

In light of these dynamics, we are forecasting a Q1 revenue range of $11.7 billion to $12.7 billion. The midpoint of $12.2 billion reflects a lower end of seasonal Q1.

- CFO David Zinsner in the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

From a numbers perspective, the next Q1 FY26 quarter is expected to post a negative YoY growth, extending the broader trend of flattish revenues for the past 3 years:

The fact that revenue has hardly grown over the last 3 years during very favorable AI related demand drivers highlights the massive missed opportunity for Intel. And unfortunately, there are no tangible signs of recovery here yet.

Another issue is that Intel's big bet on foundry has still not secured a major external customer:

We have been unsuccessful to date in securing any significant external foundry customers for any of our nodes and our prospects for securing a significant external foundry customer for Intel 14A are uncertain.

- FY25 10K.

This is a risk that continues to be an overhang on the stock. However, there is a bit more optimism on some foundry customer wins such as Apple (AAPL) in H2 FY26. But even if this materializes, the big revenue ramp from the foundry business is still at least 2 years away:

So -- in terms of 14A, realistically in terms of, I call it, the risk production in the later part of 2027 and real production, volume production in 2028. That is similar to the same time frame as a leading foundry.

- CEO Lip-Bu Tan in the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

Intel is still losing the market share battles

Intel has admitted its failures in losing ground in the data center business due to an inability to capture the AI GPU demand trends:

Our data center business has been further negatively impacted in the past few years by the significant shift of customer spend toward GPUs optimized for AI workloads, a rapidly developing and very significant compute market where we have been unsuccessful to date in becoming a meaningful participant.

- FY25 10K.

The chart below shows the extent of Intel's squandering of its leading position in the data center business in a short span of only 5 years:

And I have no reason to believe that the analyst consensus view that Intel will continue to lose to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is wrong:

Supply will improve from here, but the structural disadvantages of Intel's roadmap versus AMD are still worsening, so INTC risks missing a lot of the server market's AI-related upside.

- UBS analysts.

Note that on the CPU side too, Intel is losing share on both volume and value terms:

Overall, what this means is that the turnaround story on Intel is still very much unproven in my view. I prefer to wait for a few quarters of positive traction and reversal of these trends before considering serious buys on INTC stock.

There are multiple gross margin headwinds

Intel guided for a 277bps gross margin drop in the next quarter to 34.5%:

The company already has a negative gross margin guidance surprise vs. consensus track record and this guidance is even worse than that with a 177bps miss:

Management attributed this bleak outlook to the impact of lower revenues and maintained fixed costs in its foundry and chip development business, as well as lower yields from the next-gen CPU platform Panther Lake, which uses the 18A process node:

When you look at Q1, the gross margin decline in Q1, there's 2 main components. Obviously, revenue coming down with a largely fixed cost business is going to affect gross margins. But the other piece of it is Panther Lake, while the cost structure improves from Q4 to Q1, it's still dilutive to the corporate average, and it's a bigger percentage of the mix. So it actually has a relatively negative impact on gross margin. So that's partly why we're guiding down. There's some mixed things going on as well...

We know that 34.5% is by no means an acceptable level of gross margins. And we're actively working it, first to get it to 40% and then once we get it there, we'll move to a new target.

- CFO David Zinsner in the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

Of course, management has many words on improvement to 40% gross margins and above. But I think it is prudent to wait for proof as there are no signs of that in the numbers just yet.

INTC is trading at a lower discount vs. peers

INTC stock is trading at a 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA of 14.9x, which is about 25% below its peers' median of 19.9x:

But given the relative underperformance, it is reasonable to say that a discount valuation is warranted. But how much of a discount is too big, about right or too small? To answer this, we can look at INTC's valuation gap vs. peers over time:

The chart above shows that Intel over the past year has typically traded at a 55% discount vs. its semiconductor peers. So clearly, the current valuation gap discount of around 25% is smaller than usual. Now, I'm not sure this is really deserved as the stock's rise up has mostly been driven up by multiple expansion rather than a meaningful increase in EBITDA growth expectations:

Hence, I remain disinterested in Intel stock's valuations today.

Bullish and bearish forces seem balanced

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of INTC vs SPX500

The ratio prices in the INTC vs. S&P 500 monthly charts has reached a critical monthly resistance level and printed a big wick rejection. But the momentum leading up to this has been bullish. So given the balanced buyer and seller forces, I expect a sideways outlook ahead.

Takeaway & Positioning

I think Intel's Q4 results and commentary were lackluster. I see no signs of the same, persistent issues of revenue growth turning around, especially with supply constraints inhibiting the outlook in H1 FY26 and with uncertainty around foundry customers continuing to be an overhang on the stock. Intel has royally squandered leading positions in both the CPU and GPU markets over the last 5 years and there are no tangible signs of this trend reversing just yet. Yield pressures and negative operating leverage effects due to flattish revenues are also weighing on gross margins. Overall, management has much to prove that they can turn around this broad-based, weak operating performance momentum.

On the valuations side, I observe that INTC stock is trading at a lower discount vs peers than usual, and it has re-rated higher but without a corresponding improvement in EBITDA growth expectations. I interpret this to be a sign of some potential overvaluation and undue speculation in a successful turnaround. Technically vs. the S&P 500, INTC stock's bullish momentum has met a key monthly resistance area. I think this leads to a more neutral price action outlook ahead.

Rating: Market Perform/Hold.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
